my claude responsed to the exact same Q: Interesting idea, but let me be honest about what's actually possible before we build something. What Claude Code already does: - Autocompact automatically compresses old messages when approaching the limit (you have a 33k buffer for this) - It summarizes old turns and keeps recent ones intact What a skill can't do: - Programmatically delete or modify messages in the context window - Control when or how autocompact triggers - "Move" context to another model mid-conversation What a skill CAN do — and this is the useful part: 1. Checkpoint to disk — before autocompact loses detail, proactively save structured state (decisions made, files modified, current plan) to a file. When context gets compressed, the file survives intact. 2. Session handoff notes — when context gets heavy, generate a handoff file that a new session can @import and pick up where you left off with full fidelity. 3. Chunk markers — structure work into named chunks so that when autocompact runs, it has clean semantic boundaries to compress against instead of a wall of interleaved tool calls. The most practical version: a /checkpoint command that snapshots the current session state to a file, so you can start fresh without losing anything. You're at 17% now but after a few more sessions like this one it adds up. Want me to build that as the next skill in fiale-awesome-skills? Or were you thinking about something different — like actually routing old context through model-offload to a cheaper model for summarization?

here the final version for you Created the **`repo-arch reflex` PRD** in canvas. Small implementation summary table: | Module | Purpose | First implementation | | ------------------------- | ---------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- | | `reflex classify-command` | Allow / ask / block shell commands | Deterministic rules + package script detection | | `reflex classify-edit` | Detect risky file edits | Diff paths + fragile file scoring | | `reflex test-plan` | Recommend tests/checks | Co-change + churn + related test files | | `reflex install-pi` | Integrate with Pi auto mode | Pre-action hook returning JSON decision | | `reflex events` | Capture approvals/blocks/overrides | Append-only `.repo-arch/reflex/events.jsonl` | | `reflex dataset` | Generate training data | Export events + synthetic risky/safe examples | | `reflex train` | Train local classifier | 7B teacher → 1.5B/3B student adapter | | `session ingest` | Learn from agent failures | Convert Pi traces into lessons/guardrails | Recommended build order: 1. Policy-only command guard 2. File-edit risk classifier 3. Repo-aware fragile-file + test-plan signals 4. Pi hook 5. Dataset export 6. Teacher labeling 7. Local small-model classifier 8. Session ingestion loop - you read and h\then we can go home today

Task: Run the full autoresearch cycle on the ANE stack in the 'ane/' directory. The goal is to achieve the lowest possible val_loss on TinyStories by tuning ane/experiment_config.h. Follow the strict cycle discipline: Measure (using the baseline command from README), Identify, Implement (modifying only ane/experiment_config.h), Build (using ane/autoresearch.checks.sh), Test (re-running the baseline command), Log, and Update Strategy. The target metric is val_loss. Do not proceed until the initial baseline measurement is successfully recorded.

Run many short sprints (fresh context window every time) instead of one fragile long session. Each iteration launches the same prompt; git history + file state is the handoff. Use Opus 4.6 for planning/PRD checklists, Codex 5.3 for execution. Validate completion with markdown checklist ticking + test-driven prompts (write failing tests first). Add heartbeat monitoring + “wake hook” that pings you on finish; auto-restart on stall/crash. Run 3-4 agents in parallel via git worktrees. • Finished the requested updates and training run. - Recomputed imitation data for both classic_01 and classic_02 (the generator writes artifacts/imitation/classic_<level>.json); recorded 400 episodes each so the supervised pretrain has full-route data. - Added imitation pretraining and PPO warm-start: training/imitation_pretrain.py fits the policy to the greedy trajectories, train_puffer.py now accepts --imitation-checkpoint, and I verified the checkpoint solves classic_01 20/20 again after the scripted 12 K-step run; the new replay lives in artifacts/replays/latest_post.json. - Handed over documentation for Minimax takeover so future agents know to trigger it once limits are hit (see below). Next steps: 1. Run imitation_pretrain on classic_02 data, point PPO at that checkpoint, and verify via solve_rate before introducing enemies. 2. Expand the imitation curriculum to the no_enemies set (including shafts), then rerun PPO with the larger dataset to smooth the policy before the daemon-reactive layer. 3. Once the model is stable, start wiring daemon_feed signals back into the reward/observation stream so approvals/questions/completions can modulate emotes and reactions. Handover for Minimax takeover - Purpose: Step in when compute/time exceeds current agent limits. - Trigger: “Whenever you hit limit” (resource/time bailout in this script). - Responsibilities: continue generating imitation data, run pretraining, and carry forward PPO evaluations exactly as outlined. - Access: workspace root /Users/pavel/repos/fiale-plus/claude-session-manager, use the same venv (loderunner/.venv/bin/python). - Key commands: - python -m loderunner.training.generate_imitation_data --level classic_02 --episodes 400 --steps 140 - python -m loderunner.training.imitation_pretrain --data artifacts/imitation/classic_02.json --checkpoint artifacts/checkpoints/ imitation_classic02.pt - python -m loderunner.training.train_puffer ... --imitation-checkpoint artifacts/checkpoints/imitation_init.pt - Watch previous output: artifacts/replays/latest_post.json. If Minimax takes over, pick up at step 1 above and report solve-rate results before proceeding. can you continue here

Link: https://x.com/Suryanshti777/status/2036030768099836182Author: Suryansh Tiwari (@Suryanshti777 ) What to steal: SocratiCode repo (https://github.com/giancarloerra/SocratiCode) — zero-config, fully local semantic indexer for your entire codebase. It builds a graph of dependencies, architecture, APIs, schemas, and cross-file relationships. Agents query it instead of grep. Why it matters: Classic context engineering win. Agents stop guessing architecture and start knowing it. Direct before/after on real 2.45M-line VS Code codebase: 61.5% less context tokens, 84% fewer tool calls, 37× faster than grep-based agents. Turns every future agent run into compounding knowledge instead of repeated blind searches. Works offline with Claude Code, Cursor, MCP, Codex. Exact experiment this week: Clone the repo, run it once on your largest personal/project repo (zero config). Swap your agent’s default file-search tool for SocratiCode queries in one MCP-compatible client. Measure tool calls + context size + completion time on the same task before/after. Score: Actionability 10/10 | Novelty 8/10 | Compounding potential 10/103. Link: https://x.com/Axel_bitblaze69/status/2040312495886241836Author: Axel Bitblaze (@Axel_bitblaze69 ) What to steal: AutoAgent open-source repo (git clone the link in the post). You write one program.md describing the desired agent behavior + a tasks/ folder of benchmarks. A meta-agent then autonomously rewrites prompts, tools, routing, and config; tests against your benchmarks; keeps winners; iterates overnight inside Docker. Why it matters: This is agents writing their own tools/hooks/scripts at the meta level. Self-improving eval loop that turns manual prompt-tweaking into AutoML-for-agents. Already hit #1 on SpreadsheetBench (96.5%) and TerminalBench. Perfect for solo operators who want their coding agents to evolve without you babysitting configs. Exact experiment this week: Clone → uv sync → write a program.md for “autonomous PR reviewer that writes its own linting tools” + 3-5 benchmark tasks. Run overnight. Compare the evolved agent vs. your hand-tuned baseline on the same tasks. Score: Actionability 9/10 | Novelty 9/10 | Compounding potential 10/104. Link: https://x.com/thinkandtrade/status/2042346515549593750Author: ThinkAndTrade (@thinkandtrade ) What to steal: The full multi-agent role architecture for Claude Code + git-backed “Beads” issue tracker. Roles: Product Owner (clarifies + routes, never codes), Business Analyst (structured ACs), Story Writer (rigid story format), specialized implementers, and mandatory QA gate. One story at a time, sequential, zero tech debt. All state lives in repo files so sessions recover instantly. Why it matters: Non-obvious workflow change that turns solo context-switching hell into reliable compounding process. Parallel stories create merge/conflict debt; strict sequential + QA gate eliminates it. Git-native tracking means memory/retrieval is automatic and offline. Exactly multi-agent for a solo operator. Exact experiment this week: Set up Beads (or any file-based tracker in your repo). Implement just the PO + QA pair with two rigid prompts. Feed one real feature through the full cycle (clarify → ACs → story → implement → QA gate). Compare quality/speed vs. your normal “just ask the agent” baseline. Score: Actionability 9/10 | Novelty 8/10 | Compounding potential 9/105. Link: https://x.com/sentient_agency/status/2026974250180178420Author: Sentient (@sentient_agency ) What to steal: claude-code-best-practice repo (https://github.com/shanraisshan/claude-code-best-practice). Full production kit: persistent memory across sessions, custom hooks (trigger shell scripts on tool events), installable skills, reusable slash commands, and pre-built autonomous agent patterns. Why it matters: Turns Claude Code from chat window into a stateful autonomous coding team in one clone. Memory + hooks = compounding knowledge without you managing state. Reusable config that unlocks browser/service automation and tool-writing agents immediately. Exact experiment this week: Clone → follow the 5-minute setup. Add one custom hook that auto-commits + updates a memory.md on every successful tool use. Run your normal workflow for one day and note how many times the agent recalls past decisions without you reminding it. Score: Actionability 10/10 | Novelty 7/10 | Compounding potential 9/106. Includes examples and research references. Why it matters: Direct attack on context engineering failures. Gives agents the meta-knowledge to manage their own cognitive resources instead of you dumping monolithic .md files. Compounds because skills evolve with your repo. Exact experiment this week: Clone → drop the skills/ folder into your repo → point your agent at the context-fundamentals + multi-agent-patterns skills for one session. super cool, try setup this with /autoresearch deep Got it — you're on MLX (mlx-lm or similar). That's a strong choice for M3 Apple Silicon, especially for this MoE model (only ~3B active params). Your 30 t/s is solid, but we can push it higher with the right quant + tweaks. Quick Wins for MLX on Qwen3.6-35B-A3B (36 GB M3) Switch to Unsloth's official MLX quants (best for this exact model) Unsloth provides optimized 4-bit / 8-bit / dynamic MLX versions that load faster and run better than generic conversions. Recommended: 4-bit (fits comfortably in 36 GB with room for 128K context). Or 8-bit if you want higher quality and have headroom. Install script (one-liner from Unsloth):Bashcurl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/unslothai/unsloth/refs/heads/main/scripts/install_qwen3_6_mlx.sh | sh source ~/.unsloth/unsloth_qwen3_6_mlx/bin/activateThen load e.g.:Pythonfrom mlx_lm import load, generate model, tokenizer = load("unsloth/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-UD-MLX-4bit") # or -MLX-8bit Optimized generation call (copy-paste this as your base)Pythonresponse = generate( model=model, tokenizer=tokenizer, prompt=your_prompt, temp=0.7, top_p=0.95, top_k=40, min_p=0.1, max_tokens=16384, repetition_penalty=1.0, # For thinking/preserve mode if needed: # extra_eos_tokens=["<|im_end|>"] or use chat template with preserve_thinking )Add verbose=True during testing to see real t/s. Server mode (for OpenAI-compatible API, like your llama-server setup) Use mlx_lm.server or LM Studio with MLX backend (easiest). Or via Rapid-MLX / oMLX wrappers for extra speed knobs:Bash# Example with Rapid-MLX (if installed) rapid-mlx serve unsloth/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-UD-MLX-4bit --prefill-step-size 8192 --port 8001Higher prefill-step-size helps long-context prompt processing. Other MLX-Specific Boosts Dtype fix (big on M3): Some MLX loads default to bf16 (emulated/slower on older M chips). Force fp16 where possible for +20-25% on some setups. Speculative decoding: Available in mlx-lm but mixed results on MoE models like this one — a tiny draft (0.5-1.5B) can help dense models but sometimes slows MoE due to overhead. Test a small Qwen draft carefully; start without it. Context & memory: 128K works but monitor unified memory usage. Stick to 4-bit for stability. MLX handles unified memory very well, but prefill can still be the bottleneck at very long contexts (llama.cpp is often more stable there). Latest mlx-lm + mlx: pip install -U mlx mlx-lm — 2026 versions have big Qwen MoE improvements. Expected Speeds on M3 36 GB (Qwen3.6-35B-A3B) Good 4-bit MLX setup: 50-80+ t/s generation (short-medium context). With long 128K context: Expect some drop, but still better than baseline. If prefill feels slow → that's common in MLX; consider hybrid use (MLX for generation, llama.cpp latest for heavy long-context sessions). If you're getting exactly 30 t/s right now, share: Which exact MLX model/quant you're loading (UD-MLX-4bit etc.) Your current generate/server command Typical context length in use I can give you a more precise tuned script or debug the bottleneck. MLX is already one of the fastest paths on your hardware — we're just polishing it. (If you actually meant you're not on MLX and want to stick with llama.cpp, let me know and we'll double down on the speculative + latest build from before.) - and run it to find better setup

grok suggestions **Thread summary (llama.cpp-focused flags for Qwen 3.6-35B-A3B on low VRAM):** The main post + replies break down why people get surprisingly fast TPS with this MoE model (35B total params, only ~3B active per token) even on modest hardware: the “trick” isn’t just 4-bit weights — it’s aggressively shrinking the **KV cache** (the real memory killer for long context) while keeping the model fast. Key flags everyone is praising (from the explained `llama-server` command + replies): - **-c 131072** (or your target context) + **--fit on** + **--fit-target 256** + **--fit-ctx 131072** → lets the model spill across unified RAM/GPU intelligently and keeps some headroom free. - **-fa on** → Flash Attention (big speed win). - **-ctk q8_0** and **-ctv q8_0** (or even lower with TheTom’s fork + `ctv=turbo3`) → 8-bit (or asymmetric/turbo) KV cache instead of FP16/BF16. This is the biggest memory saver; KV cache can easily eat more RAM than the weights on long chats. - **--no-context-shift** → prevents auto-trimming old context. - **--np 1** (or `--parallel X` + `--no-kv-unified`) → stops cache memory from exploding with multiple slots. - **--n-gpu-layers 99** → full GPU offload where possible. - MoE-specific: offload some experts to CPU (better than dropping KV quant from Q8 → Q4 in some cases). - Bonus speed: `--spec-type ngram-simple` (free speculative decoding, great for structured/tool outputs) or TheTom’s llama.cpp fork with turbo quant. Result: people report solid TPS on 12–32 GB VRAM setups because KV cache stops being the bottleneck. The image in the thread literally labels every flag so you can copy-paste and tweak. **Your exact problem (M3 36 GB + Qwen 3.6 35B-A3B 4-quant) + mlx-lm solution:** mlx-lm is the **right tool** for M3 — it’s native Apple Silicon and routinely hits 40+ TPS where llama.cpp tops out around 30. The crash is 100% the unbounded KV cache (classic mlx_lm.server / generate issue on Apple Silicon; it just keeps growing until Metal OOM/kernel panic). **Fixes that actually work (tested in the wild on M3/M4 with similar 30B+ MoE models):** 1. **Cap the KV cache explicitly** (this is the #1 thing you need): ```bash mlx_lm.generate \ --model mlx-community/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit \ # or your converted path --max-kv-size 8192 \ # or 16384 / 32768 — start here and tune --max-tokens 4096 \ # or whatever output limit you want ... ``` For the server: ```bash mlx_lm.server --model ... --max-kv-size 16384 --port 8080 ``` (Support landed in mlx-lm ~0.31+; if your version is older, `pip install -U mlx-lm`.) 2. **Even tighter memory control**: - `--prompt-cache-bytes 8589934592` (8 GB cap) or `--prompt-cache-size 32768` - Pre-compute a prompt cache for long/repetitive sessions: `mlx_lm.cache_prompt --prompt-cache-file cache.safetensors ...` then reuse it. Huge win for coding/agent loops. 3. **Extra speed tweaks that stack with the above**: - Use an official/community mlx-converted 4-bit model (mlx-community/Qwen3.6-...-4bit on HF) — they’re already optimized. - If you need even lower memory: try the dynamic/ learned quant options or Q4 KV cache (mlx-lm supports quantized KV now). - Keep context reasonable (8k–16k for chat/coding is plenty for most people; 128k is overkill and kills speed). - `--chat-template-args '{"preserve_thinking": false}'` or whatever Qwen-specific args you need. With `--max-kv-size` set so the cache never exceeds ~12–18 GB (leaving headroom on your 36 GB M3), you should get the full 40+ TPS **without crashing**. People are running the exact same 35B-A3B model on M3/M4 hardware at those speeds once the cache is bounded. **Quick test command you can copy** (adjust model path): ```bash mlx_lm.generate --model mlx-community/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit \ --prompt "Your prompt here" \ --max-kv-size 16384 \ --max-tokens 2048 \ --temp 0.7 --top-p 0.95 ``` Drop the TPS numbers you get after adding `--max-kv-size` and I can help tune the exact value for your workflow. You’re literally one flag away from stable 40+ TPS on mlx-lm. 🚀 , anything we did not try
